studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at three time points: prior to intervention, after completion of the intervention (9 weeks later), and at follow-up (6 months later).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received immediate access to the web-based intervention Deprexis, while the control arm (wait-list group) received access to Deprexis after 6 months.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for severity of depressive symptoms (primary outcome), WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF), Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (QIDS), Core Self-Evaluations Scale (CSES), Suicidal Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Hamburg MS Quality of Life Questionnaire (HALEMS).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received access to the computerized self-help program for depression (Deprexis) for 8 weeks. The control group was a wait-list control condition, waiting 8 weeks before accessing the program.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16) administered every 2 weeks during treatment; Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) completed pre- and post-treatment; Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS) completed pre- and post-treatment; Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ) completed pre- and post-treatment; Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ) completed pre-treatment; Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ) completed pre-treatment; Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) completed pre- and post-treatment.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Unguided self-help group received a web-based self-help program without therapist support. Guided self-help group received the same program with weekly therapist e-mail support. Wait-list control group received access to the program after 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory - II (BDI-II), Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI), Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (64-item IIP), WHOQOL-BREF, CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Symptoms of depression and other aspects were assessed over a one-year period. Controls received online self-help after the one-year follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = Deprexis (online self-help with and without email support), C = waiting-list control
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an online self-help program (Deprexis) with and without email support. The control group was a waiting-list control that received the intervention after the one-year follow-up.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Personal Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks. Secondary Outcomes: Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Personal Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 6 months and 12 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received the web-based intervention Deprexis, consisting of ten online modules with cognitive-behavioral techniques. The control arm was a wait-list group that received access to Deprexis after eight weeks.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for severity of depressive symptoms; WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) for quality of life.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed three months after randomization; secondary outcomes assessed at various points including six months after randomization.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = deprexis® group, C = Information group
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group used deprexis® for 90 days as an additional treatment during inpatient stay and aftercare intervention. Control group received weekly online information about depression for 90 days as a placebo comparator.
studies.collected_data: Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE), European Health Interview Survey Quality of Life-8 (EUROHIS-QOL 8), Work Ability Index (WAI), self-devised questionnaires for acceptance/utilization, Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ), OPD-Structure Questionnaire (OPD-SFK), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), mood/depressiveness/utilization of units, utilization of other treatments, satisfaction with inpatient treatment, willingness to pay, remission from depression (BDI score below cutoff of 13 and Reliable Change Index).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received a stepped care model (SCM) with treatments tailored to depression severity, including active monitoring, bibliotherapy, online self-help, outpatient psychotherapy, psychiatric treatment, and combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment. The control group received treatment as usual from general physicians or other health service providers.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-D-9 depression scale, cost-benefit ratio in QALYs, response/remission/relapse (PHQ-D-9), quality of life (EQ-5D, SF-12), health service utilization and medication consumption, Rosenberg self-esteem scale, Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), PHQ-D anxiety scale, PHQ-D panic symptoms, PHQ-D somatisation, GAD-7 general anxiety disorder scale.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received Deprexis, an internet-delivered psychosocial treatment for depressive symptoms and related functional impairment. The control group received treatment-as-usual (TAU), which included access to standard non-study care.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report; QIDS-SR-16) to measure depressive symptom severity, World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS) to assess functional disability, and Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) to measure symptom-related disability. Data collected at baseline, post-treatment (week 8), and follow-up (week 16).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants will be assessed at baseline (T0), mid-intervention (4 weeks, T1), post-intervention (8 weeks, T2), and follow-up (3 months post-intervention, T3).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 160 (80 in each experimental group) and C = 80
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group: Online self-help program for depression (Deprexis). Second Experimental group: Online self-help program (Deprexis) plus peer-support via an online forum. Control group: Waitlist control group with access to the program after 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 for depressive symptoms, Fribourg Reward Task for reward responses, SHAPS for pleasure experience, BIS/BAS for reward/punishment sensitivity, PSS for perceived social support, GAD-7 for anxiety symptoms, GSES for self-efficacy, SF-12 for quality of life, RSE for self-esteem, PSS-Short Version for stress, SSS-8 for somatic symptoms, PCL-5 for PTSD symptoms, CTQ-SF for childhood trauma, SUS for usability, CSQ for client satisfaction.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The primary trial phase lasted 3 months, followed by long-term online follow-up at 6 and 12 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 186 (Deprexis and DeprexisPlus combined) and C = 93 (Waitlist Control)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis group received access to the web-based CBT program. DeprexisPlus group received the same program with additional weekly email support. Waitlist Control group waited 6 months before receiving access to the program.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II), WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF), Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS), Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC), Chalder Fatigue Scale, Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS), Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview (M.I.N.I.), clinical assessments at baseline and month 3.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: Online self-help; CAU: Care as usual
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items (PHQ-9), Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms - Self-Report (QIDS-SR16), Generalized Anxiety Disorder - 7 (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire - 15 items (PHQ-15), Short Form Health Survey - 12 (SF-12)

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group used the Deprexis program for 12 weeks, while the control group did not have access to the program.
studies.collected_data: Depression severity: PHQ-9; Quality of life: SF12 and EQ-5D; Social functioning: WSAS; Cost data from DAK (excluding ambulant costs); Satisfaction with DAK services and acceptance of Deprexis program.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the internet-based intervention 'Deprexis' for 8 weeks, consisting of ten modules based on cognitive-behavioral therapy. Wait-list control group did not receive any additional treatment during the intervention period but gained access to 'Deprexis' after the post-assessment.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for pathological gambling, Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS) for gambling severity, General Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7) for anxiety levels, Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ) for common mental disorders (baseline only), and subjective appraisal of the intervention (post-assessment only).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline (T1) and after 12 weeks (T4) for all groups. Additional interim assessments were conducted at 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3) for the supported intervention group (IG-A).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: IG-A: Supported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® and brief therapeutic consultations at 4, 8, and 12 weeks. IG-B: Unsupported intervention group with DiGA deprexis® only. CG: Waitlist control group receiving no intervention during the 12-week period but gaining access to DiGA deprexis® afterward.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcomes: Reduction in depressive symptoms (PHQ-9), anxiety symptoms (GAD-7), and global psychological distress (SCL-90-R). Secondary outcomes: Acceptance (e.g., satisfaction, perceived fit), usage intensity, adherence, dropout rates, reasons for dropout, subjectively perceived effects and burdens. Exploratory outcomes: Comparison of supported vs unsupported DiGA use, moderator and predictor variables (e.g., sociodemographics, baseline symptoms, attitudes toward digital health applications), and evaluation of DiGA's relevance as a bridging intervention.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus web-based online platform (deprexis). Control group: Psychotherapy without Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) somatic symptom module, Short-Form Health Survey-12 (SF-12), Questionnaire for the evaluation of psychotherapeutic progress (FEP-2), Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ), Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Psychological empowerment scale (adapted version), Depression diagnosis by licensed psychotherapists.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were assessed at three different time points: before receiving access to the treatment, after 10 weeks, and after 6 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One group received immediate access to the web-based treatment (deprexis) after giving written informed consent, while the other group received access after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Depression levels by BDI-II, clinical depression by Patient Health Questionnaire and clinical interview (SCID); stress perception by Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ); social support by ENRICHD Social Support Inventory (ESSI); quality of life by WHOQOL-BREF; health behavior; client satisfaction by Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks, 18 weeks, and 6 months after program usage termination.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received access to the Deprexis® program, consisting of ten interactive modules completed over nine weeks. The control group did not receive access to the program during the first phase.
studies.collected_data: German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation), subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis; control group was a waitlist.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS) assessing symptoms such as sadness, indecision, early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved sadness and indecision, which were associated with changes in other symptoms such as early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. Most symptoms showed decreases compared to the waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons, except for agitation and early insomnia. Six somatic symptoms were not impacted by Deprexis.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Post-discharge, patients were followed by structured telephone interviews up to study-endpoint, i.e., 12 weeks after deprexis-initiation.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: TAU group received treatment-as-usual, while TAU-PLUS group received treatment-as-usual plus guided deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Beck-Depression-Inventory-II (BDI-II). Secondary outcomes: Hamilton-Depression-Scale, Clinical-Global-Impression-Severity, WHO-Well-Being-Index, Helping-Alliance-Questionnaire. Other measures: working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, utilization of post-hospital care after discharge.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, TAU-PLUS patients showed significant improvement in BDI-II scores (p=.03, d=-.73), greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70), earlier discharge (p=.003), lower rate of post-hospital care (p=.01), and fewer re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcomes and alliance with therapists showed no significant differences between groups.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week online intervention (deprexis). Control group received a control condition.
studies.collected_data: Statutory health insurance costs (using health insurers' administrative data), depression severity, health-related quality of life, impairment in functioning (assessed via patient self-report at baseline, post-treatment, and three-months' and nine-months' follow-up).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total costs of statutory health insurance decreased by 32% in the intervention group (from 3139€ per year at baseline to 2119€ in the study year) compared to a 13% reduction in the control group. The intervention group showed significantly greater reductions in depression severity and impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants in the control group received delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks, and the intervention group had access to Deprexis for 90 days.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days. Control group received treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis after 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depression self-assessment, Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM) for general psychological state, perceived self-efficacy measure, and satisfaction with Deprexis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the intervention group improved significantly more than the control group on PHQ-9 (Cohen d=0.80; P<.001), CORE-OM (Cohen d=0.82; P<.001), and perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission rates were 21% in the intervention group compared to 7% in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the intervention group, and participants reported medium to high satisfaction with Deprexis.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received an 8-week course of Deprexis immediately; control group was waitlisted for 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR), interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, depression-related disability, symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for secondary outcomes such as interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy alone.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 12 weeks; secondary outcomes included anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life at 12 weeks and six months follow-up; working alliance assessed at six and 12 weeks.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks, with medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07-.55). No negative side effects, such as a lower working alliance, were observed in the blended format.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received 9 weeks of immediate-program-access as an add-on to treatment-as-usual. Control group received 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) for depression severity as the primary outcome measure. Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) for social functioning as the secondary outcome measure. Measurements were taken at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and 6-month follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reduction in depression severity (BDI) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94) and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA) with Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95) at 9 weeks. Improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses showed significant effects on depression (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55) and social functioning (WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). Higher percentage of intervention group experienced significant reduction in depression symptoms (BDI: OR = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovery (OR = 17.3, CI 95% = 2.3 - 130). Over 80% of users found the program helpful.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received regular psychotherapy plus the web-based depression program ('deprexis'), while the control group received regular psychotherapy only.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms at 3 months post-randomization (primary outcome). Secondary outcomes include changes in anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, working alliance, and feasibility/acceptability of the treatment condition. All outcomes are assessed again at 6 months post-randomization.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants received TILS or sham/placebo once a week for 4 weeks, starting after the first 2 weeks of the 12-week Deprexis program.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis combined with TILS; control group received Deprexis combined with sham/placebo.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) scores to measure depressive symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The sham group showed a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores, while the TILS group showed a 56% reduction, representing an additional 30% reduction in depressive symptoms with TILS. No adverse effects were reported.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were re-assessed after 8 weeks.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the online depression-focused intervention 'Deprexis', while the control group was on a wait-list.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS), South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) for gambling-related symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive and gambling-related symptoms compared to the control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. Deprexis was particularly beneficial for participants with high baseline gambling-related symptoms and those gambling due to loneliness.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Intermediary and long-term assessments were conducted over 36 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis), while the control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Symptom-specific effects of depression, item-level effects on depressive symptoms (e.g., worthlessness, fatigue, depressed mood, concentration, activity level), aspects of quality of life (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), baseline scores predicting symptom reduction, network intervention analysis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Internet interventions for depression (Deprexis) are effective in the short term and exert long-term effects. Effects are likely to impact a small subset of problems, with patients reporting these problems benefiting more from the intervention. The configuration of direct and indirect effects replicates findings from another study examining the same intervention.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week web-based cognitive behavioral intervention (deprexis) in addition to care as usual. Control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, direct health care costs. Outcomes were assessed via patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between groups. Outpatient treatment costs showed numeric differences, with psychotherapeutic treatment costs decreasing in the intervention group (-16.8% (€80)) and increasing in the control group (+14.7% (€60)) (tdf=685=2.57; p=0.008).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the Deprexis online cognitive behavioural therapy programme; control group was a waitlist control group.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) scores, occurrence of depressive symptoms above clinical cutoff (BDI >13), occurrence of suicidal ideation.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group, with a mean group difference of -4.02 points (95% CI -7.26 to -0.79, p=0.015, effect size d=0.53). Worsening of depressive symptoms above the clinical cutoff occurred in 7% of the control group and 0% of the Deprexis group. No adverse events related to suicidal ideation were noted.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Care as usual (CAU) vs CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis.
studies.collected_data: Depression symptoms pre and post intervention, patient characteristics, self-rating screening for mental disorders, attitudes towards online interventions, quality of life, adherence metrics (usage time and number of sessions).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher adherence to the program predicted greater symptom reduction in depressive symptoms over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14). Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Receiving guidance was strongly associated with symptom reduction (β = .27 and .26).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis plus care as usual (CAU), while control group received CAU alone.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms. Measurements were taken at baseline, 12 weeks, and at 6 and 12 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences in post-test scores indicated the effectiveness of Deprexis. No sociodemographic variables were found to moderate treatment outcomes. Deprexis was equally beneficial across all subgroups.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group accessed the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual. Control group had access to care-as-usual.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, Web Screening Questionnaire for anxiety-related comorbidities (generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Social phobia was found to moderate the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis. The moderating effect of social phobia did not depend on other moderators in the model. Results were significant (p < .05) but did not reach small effect sizes, accounted for less than 1% of the variance in depressive symptom change, and showed limited reproducibility in split-half analyses.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I), Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8), Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI)
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The results suggested a two-factor solution, with a very good model fit and evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and the sub-scales. The WAI-I was found to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Early change patterns were assessed in the first four weeks, and remission was tracked over 36 months.
studies.collected_data: Baseline variables, early change patterns in the first four weeks, remission onset over 36 months, Stepwise Cox Proportional Hazard Multiple Regression analysis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Early response after registration and after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission compared to early deterioration. Early improvement in depression symptoms predicted long-term remission.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 completed biweekly during treatment
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Four change-dropout patterns were identified: (1) High impairment, improvement, and low dropout probability (C3, N = 134) with the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up; (2) High impairment, deterioration, and high dropout probability (C2, N = 32); (3) Low impairment, improvement, and high dropout probability (C1, N = 198); (4) High impairment, no change, and low dropout probability (C4, N = 119). Rapid improvement may lead to dropout due to perceived 'good enough' dosage of treatment.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis (online intervention for depression) and control group received care as usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Compared to Millennials, Boomers used the intervention significantly more often (d = 0.45) and for a longer duration (d = 0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d = 0.14). No significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. Post-assessment between-group effect sizes for Millennials and Boomers were d = 0.26 and d = 0.39, respectively, and were stable at follow-up (d = 0.37 and d = 0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and comparably effective among Millennials and Baby Boomers.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 1013 (Deprexis® intervention group) and C = care as usual
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the internet-based intervention Deprexis®, while the control group received care as usual. Both groups were permitted to use any type of treatment.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) assessing nine depressive symptoms, treatment condition, changes in symptoms, network estimation techniques.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Seven of nine depressive symptoms showed larger improvements in the intervention group compared to care as usual, with effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31. No significant differences were found for the other two symptoms. Network estimation suggested that four symptom-specific effects were direct, while three were indirect. Exploratory analyses indicated greater effectiveness for participants with higher scores on the four directly affected symptoms.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention period lasted 3 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received deprexis; control group received care as usual.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received the Internet-based program deprexis, while the control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions, efficacy of the program deprexis, changes in attitudes from pre to post intervention, perceived lack of personal contact, and usage time.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014), while negative attitudes were associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). Attitudes changed during the trial, and the magnitude and direction of change were associated with program efficacy over time (η2p = .030).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care, while the control group received care as usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for self-rated depression severity, assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment), and 6 months (follow-up).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). Post-assessment between-group effect size was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64), stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). Rate of participants experiencing minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%), with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Standard psychotherapy group (control) and augmented therapy group (intervention) with a 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis).
studies.collected_data: Symptoms of depression, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life, rates of clinical improvement.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Combined treatment led to greater reduction in symptoms of depression (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). No intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. Adjunctive treatment resulted in increased rates of clinical improvement, and treatment success was independent of therapeutic orientation.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) was assessed regularly over twelve months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) + usual care, C = care as usual alone
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care, while the control group received care as usual alone.
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9) was assessed regularly over twelve months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates were significantly higher in the intervention group (HR 1.31; p = 0.009). The intervention was more effective in participants not taking antidepressant medication (HR 1.88; p < 0.001). PHQ-change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (p < 0.001), with the between-group effect decreasing from d = 0.36 at three months to d = 0.13 at twelve months (group by time interaction: p < 0.001).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.intervention_and_control_group: I = 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) + usual care, C = care-as-usual alone
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care, while the control arm received care-as-usual alone.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depression severity, demographic and clinical parameters, Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction=0.28, p=0.84). The intervention was effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Duration of treatment and assessments: 12 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group (IG) used lancivis alongside treatment as usual (TAU), while control group (CG) received TAU only.
studies.collected_data: Quality of life (QoL), cancer-related fatigue, distress symptoms, fear of cancer progression. Assessments conducted at 3 months (T1) and 6 months (T2).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant improvements in fatigue in the IG compared to the CG at 3 months (Cohen’s d: 0.29) and 6 months (Cohen’s d: 0.31). IG reported significantly lower fear of cancer progression at T1 and T2 and lower levels of distress symptoms at T2. No between-group differences were found for health-related QoL.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Observation period of 3 months with four assessment points: baseline (start of therapy), 3 weeks, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants used the deprexis®24 online psychotherapy program, with or without antidepressive comedication.
studies.collected_data: Short version Montgomery Asberg Depression Scale (svMADRS), Clinical Global Impression-Severity/Improvement (CGI-S / CGI-I), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS), Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-4), compliance questionnaires, internet usage questionnaires, and standardized documentation of safety-related incidents and adverse events.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline as well as at weeks 3, 6, 9 and 12.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received 12-week access to the self-management intervention (deprexis) in addition to usual care.
studies.collected_data: Clinician-rated short version of the Montgomery Asberg-Depression Scale (svMADRS), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), usage data (number of sessions).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Severity of depressive symptoms decreased significantly over the observation period: svMADRS scores reduced from 29.72 (SD = 10.03) to 15.73 (SD = 9.74) with Cohen's d = 1.42 (95% CI 0.08-2.76); PHQ-9 scores reduced from 15.20 (SD = 5.03) to 8.77 (SD = 5.03) with Cohen's d = 1.29 (95% CI 0.60-1.97).
